children.text: This chapter argues that riverine trade guilds sustained collective bargaining power through rotating stewardship rather than fixed leadership, a claim building on Renwick, A. (1990) but departing from the static-leadership model that literature otherwise assumes.
children.label: text

children.text: The scope of the argument is limited to riverine guilds active between the founding charter and the second reorganization, drawing on guild ledgers rather than external chronicles.
children.label: text

children.text: This body section is illustrative only and is not itself part of the envelope evidence this fixture exercises; the structural-envelope pass reads the introduction and conclusion, not this body section.
children.label: text

children.text: In sum, rotating stewardship better explains the guild's durable bargaining power than fixed leadership, restating and extending the position outlined in the introduction and echoing the reading in Okafor, D. (2003).
children.label: text
